C:
The faster version uses a `Set` for O(n) instead of O(n²):

```javascript
function dedup(a){ return [...new Set(a)]; }
```

**Improvement:** O(n²) → O(n). The original calls `a.indexOf(x)` for every element, and each `indexOf` scans from the start of the array — so n elements × up-to-n scans = quadratic. A `Set` does each membership check in amortized O(1), so the whole thing is linear. On a 100k-element array that's roughly the difference between ~10 billion operations and ~100k.

One behavioral note worth knowing: the two aren't byte-for-byte identical on `NaN`. The original drops all `NaN` values (because `NaN === NaN` is false, so `indexOf` never matches), while `[...new Set(a)]` keeps a single `NaN` (Set uses SameValueZero equality). For every other value type the results match, and the Set version's handling of `NaN` is the more intuitive one. If you specifically need the old NaN-dropping behavior, say so and I'll give you a variant that preserves it.
